title: Grafton Elliot Smith

infobox.value: (1871-08-15)15 August 1871  [Grafton, New South Wales](/wiki/Grafton,_New_South_Wales "Grafton, New South Wales")
[University of Sydney](/wiki/University_of_Sydney "University of Sydney"), [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge")
[Royal Medal](/wiki/Royal_Medal "Royal Medal") (1912) [Fellow of the Royal Society](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society")

(15 August 1871 – 1 January 1937) was an [Australian](/wiki/Australia "Australia")-[British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") [anatomist](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy"), [Egyptologist](/wiki/Egyptology "Egyptology") and a proponent of the [hyperdiffusionist view of prehistory](/wiki/Hyperdiffusionism_in_archaeology "Hyperdiffusionism in archaeology").[[1]](#cite_note-frs-1) He believed in the idea that cultural innovations occur only once and that they spread geographically. Based on this, he traced the origins of many cultural and traditional practices across the world, including the New World, to ideas that he believed came from Egypt and in some instances from Asia. An expert on brain anatomy, he was one of the first to study Egyptian mummies using radiological techniques. He took an interest in extinct humanoids and was embroiled in controversy over the authenticity of the [Piltdown Man](/wiki/Piltdown_Man "Piltdown Man").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Anatomist and egyptologist

| [Professor](/wiki/Professor "Professor")  Sir Grafton Elliot Smith  [FRS](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society") [FRSE](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh "Fellow of the Royal Society of Edinburgh") [FRCP](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_College_of_Physicians "Fellow of the Royal College of Physicians") | |
| --- | --- |
| Grafton Elliot Smith | |

| Died | 1 January 1937(1937-01-01) (aged 65)  [Broadstairs](/wiki/Broadstairs "Broadstairs"), [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"), [England](/wiki/England "England") |
| Alma mater | [University of Sydney](/wiki/University_of_Sydney "University of Sydney"), [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") |
| Spouse | Kathleen Macreadie  ​ (m. 1902)​ |
| Awards | [Royal Medal](/wiki/Royal_Medal "Royal Medal") (1912) [Fellow of the Royal Society](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society") |

|  | |

**Sir Grafton Elliot Smith** (15 August 1871 – 1 January 1937) was an [Australian](/wiki/Australia "Australia")-[British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") [anatomist](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy"), [Egyptologist](/wiki/Egyptology "Egyptology") and a proponent of the [hyperdiffusionist view of prehistory](/wiki/Hyperdiffusionism_in_archaeology "Hyperdiffusionism in archaeology").[[1]](#cite_note-frs-1) He believed in the idea that cultural innovations occur only once and that they spread geographically. Based on this, he traced the origins of many cultural and traditional practices across the world, including the New World, to ideas that he believed came from Egypt and in some instances from Asia. An expert on brain anatomy, he was one of the first to study Egyptian mummies using radiological techniques. He took an interest in extinct humanoids and was embroiled in controversy over the authenticity of the [Piltdown Man](/wiki/Piltdown_Man "Piltdown Man").

## Professional career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_Elliot_Smith&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Professional career")]

Smith was born in [Grafton, New South Wales](/wiki/Grafton,_New_South_Wales "Grafton, New South Wales") to Stephen Sheldrick Smith who had moved to Australia from London in 1860 and Mary Jane, née Evans. He received his early education from Grafton Public School where his father was headmaster. When the family moved to Sydney in 1883 he went to Darlington Public School before joining [Sydney Boys High School](/wiki/Sydney_Boys_High_School "Sydney Boys High School"). He attended evening classes on physiology by Thomas Anderson Stuart and took an early interest in biology. In an autobiographical note Smith noted that Stuart had shown them the convolutions of the human brain and declared that nobody understood them fully. Smith decided at that point that he would work towards understanding them. Accordingly, he went to study medicine at the [University of Sydney](/wiki/University_of_Sydney "University of Sydney") in 1888 and received a Doctor of Medicine in 1895, with a dissertation on the fore-brain of the [monotremes](/wiki/Monotremes "Monotremes")[[2]](#cite_note-2)) and developed an interest in the anatomy of the human brain. He received a James King travelling scholarship and went to [St John's College, Cambridge](/wiki/St_John%27s_College,_Cambridge "St John's College, Cambridge"), graduating BA in 1896. Afterwards he catalogued the human [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain")-collection of the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum").[[3]](#cite_note-adb-3)

Smith obtained an appointment at the [Cairo School of Medicine](/w/index.php?title=Cairo_School_of_Medicine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cairo School of Medicine (page does not exist)") in 1900 on the suggestion of his anthropologist friend Alexander Macalister. Smith became [archaeological](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") advisor to the archaeological survey of [Nubia](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia") in the wake of plans to construct the [Aswan Dam](/wiki/Aswan_Dam "Aswan Dam") which threatened to drown numerous archaeological sites. Smith conducted investigations on the [brains](/wiki/Brain "Brain") of [Egyptian](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") [mummies](/wiki/Mummy "Mummy"). He was one of the first to non-destructively use [x-rays](/wiki/X-ray "X-ray") to study mummies.[[4]](#cite_note-todd-4) Smith took a special interest in the pathologies indicated in ancient skeletal remains. He noted for instance that many Egyptian skulls had biparietal thinning which had been common in European aristocrats in the past. Smith hypothesized this was the result of wearing heavy wigs or headgear. From 1909 to 1919 he was Professor in anatomy in [Manchester](/wiki/University_of_Manchester "University of Manchester"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) 1919–1937 he held the chair of Anatomy at the [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London"). He was elected President of the [Anatomical Society of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/Anatomical_Society_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "Anatomical Society of Great Britain and Ireland") for 1924 to 1927.[[6]](#cite_note-6) During World War I he took an interest in the neurology of [shell shock](/wiki/Shell_shock "Shell shock"), visiting military hospitals and serving on the British [General Medical Council](/wiki/General_Medical_Council "General Medical Council").[[7]](#cite_note-waldron-7)[[3]](#cite_note-adb-3)

Smith was the leading specialist on the evolution of the brain of his day.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Many of his ideas on the evolution of the primate brain still form the core of present scholarship. He proposed the following stages of development:

1. a smell-dominated insectivore of the jumping [shrew](/wiki/Shrew "Shrew")-type
2. vision-dominated animals with an expanded cortex of tree-shrew-type
3. acutely visioned, manually dexterous mammals of tarsius-type
4. monkeys
5. anthropoids using their hands to use and produce tools

## Honours and awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_Elliot_Smith&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Honours and awards")]

Smith was decorated by the [Khedive](/wiki/Khedive "Khedive") of Egypt, [Abbas Hilmy](/wiki/Abbas_II_of_Egypt "Abbas II of Egypt") in 1909 with an Insignia of the Third Class of the Imperial Ottoman Order of the Osmaniah.[[9]](#cite_note-9) He became Fellow of the [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society") in 1907,[[1]](#cite_note-frs-1) [FRCP](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_College_of_Physicians "Fellow of the Royal College of Physicians"), elected to membership of the [Manchester Literary and Philosophical Society](/wiki/Manchester_Literary_and_Philosophical_Society "Manchester Literary and Philosophical Society") on 4 October 1910,[[10]](#cite_note-10) President of the Society 1919, cross of the French Legion of Honour, and was knighted in the [1934 Birthday Honours](/wiki/1934_Birthday_Honours "1934 Birthday Honours").[[11]](#cite_note-11) In 1912 he received the Royal Medal of the Royal Society, in 1930 the Honorary Gold Medal of the [Royal College of Surgeons](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Surgeons_of_England "Royal College of Surgeons of England"), in 1936 the [Huxley Memorial Medal](/wiki/Huxley_Memorial_Medal "Huxley Memorial Medal") from the [Royal Anthropological Institute of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/Royal_Anthropological_Institute_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "Royal Anthropological Institute of Great Britain and Ireland").[[3]](#cite_note-adb-3)

## European hypothesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_Elliot_Smith&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: European hypothesis")]

British anthropologists [Arthur Keith](/wiki/Arthur_Keith "Arthur Keith") and Grafton Elliot Smith both supported the European origin of humankind as opposed to models of Asian and African origin.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In several of his works, Smith argued that Europe was the cradle of humanity, identifying a European [Mediterranean race](/wiki/Mediterranean_race "Mediterranean race") as the occupants of the original home of modern humans. His cradle was large, as he claimed the Mediterranean race had occupied the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") and western Europe, including the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"). He especially linked the Mediterranean race to the civilisation of Egypt. Smith's arguments later became known as his theory of [diffusionism](/wiki/Diffusionism "Diffusionism").[[13]](#cite_note-13) According to Smith and [William James Perry](/wiki/W._J._Perry "W. J. Perry"), Egypt was the source of all cultural innovations and the ultimate source of human civilisation.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

According to Smith, "Man did not become truly erect until his brain had developed in a very particular way to make it possible for him to use his hands". That line of reasoning reinforced the European origin of human, which Smith and Keith supported, as the mostly large brained specimens such as the [Cro-Magnon](/wiki/Cro-Magnon "Cro-Magnon") had been found in Europe.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Hyperdiffusionism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_Elliot_Smith&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Hyperdiffusionism")]

The term '[hyperdiffusionism](/wiki/Hyperdiffusionism "Hyperdiffusionism")' seems to have been coined by the British archaeologist [Glyn Daniel](/wiki/Glyn_Daniel "Glyn Daniel") in his book *The Idea of Prehistory* (1962) with a somewhat derogatory intention. It was intended to represent extremes of [diffusionism](/wiki/Diffusionism "Diffusionism"), a theme popular in early 20th century archaeology that itself has been subject to criticism. Smith believed that all megalithic phenomena, whether in Northwestern [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") or [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica"), had originated in Ancient Egypt. "Small groups of people, moving mainly by sea, settled at certain places and there made rude imitations of the Egyptian monuments of the [Pyramid](/wiki/Pyramid "Pyramid") Age." (Smith 1911, ix). Smith believed in a direct [diffusion](/wiki/Diffusion_(anthropology) "Diffusion (anthropology)") to [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), [Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete"), East [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), Southern [Arabia](/wiki/Arabian_peninsula "Arabian peninsula") and [Sumer](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer"), and other areas were influenced by secondary diffusion. The [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") culture of Europe was derived from Egypt as well, according to Smith. He even interpreted a mummy from a [Torres Strait](/wiki/Torres_Strait "Torres Strait") island as definitely being indicative of an Egyptian influence.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The concept of hyperdiffusionism is now referred to by more neutral terms (when referring to the Americas) such as [Pre-Columbian trans-oceanic contact](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_trans-oceanic_contact "Pre-Columbian trans-oceanic contact").

Smith interpreted a small carving detail in Copán [stela](/wiki/Maya_stelae "Maya stelae") B as an elephant, an animal unknown in the New World. [Alfred Maudslay](/wiki/Alfred_Maudslay "Alfred Maudslay") had described the detail as being a stylised tapir. Smith argued that the carving shows an elephant with a mahout atop it. He went on to point out many Asian features in the sculpture in his book *Elephants and ethnologists* (1924).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

#### Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_Elliot_Smith&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Egypt")]

[Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") held a fortunate geographical position that made contacts to western [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") possible, while being safe from [invasions](/wiki/Invasion "Invasion"). The fertile soil led to ample leisure, in art and the crafts could be cultivated. Smith believed that agriculture had originated in Egypt and only later spread to [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"). "The earliest cultivators of the soil in Egypt were in fact laying the foundations not merely of [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") and [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation") but of all the [arts](/wiki/Art "Art") and craft, the [social](/wiki/Society "Society") organization and [religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion") beliefs which became an integral part of the civilization that was being built up sixty centuries ago and in later ages was diffused throughout the world." (Smith 1911, 6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Grafton_Elliot_Smith_Cultural_Diffusion_Map_from_Egypt.jpg/500px-Grafton_Elliot_Smith_Cultural_Diffusion_Map_from_Egypt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grafton_Elliot_Smith_Cultural_Diffusion_Map_from_Egypt.jpg)

Cultural diffusion map from Egypt by Grafton Elliot Smith (1929).

Artificial irrigation led to cooperation and the development of a central [government](/wiki/Government "Government") that was based on professional knowledge, a rule of hydraulic [engineers](/wiki/Engineers "Engineers"). The prosperity of everybody depended on a successful administration and a strong central government (cf. [Karl Wittfogel](/wiki/Karl_Wittfogel "Karl Wittfogel")'s hydraulic hypothesis). Later on, the leading engineer became a [sacred king](/wiki/Sacred_king "Sacred king") (cf. [Henri Frankfort](/wiki/Henri_Frankfort "Henri Frankfort")) and a [god](/wiki/God "God") ([Osiris](/wiki/Osiris "Osiris")) after death. [Ritual](/wiki/Ritual "Ritual") and [magic](/wiki/Magic_and_religion "Magic and religion") formed the germs of the first [sciences](/wiki/Science "Science"), of [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology") and [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"). The building of tombs initiated the development of [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture").

Other inventions of the [Egyptians](/wiki/Egyptians "Egyptians") were:

* Weaving
* Metal working (gold and copper)
* Calendar
* Seagoing ships
* "Art of shaving"
* Wigs
* Hats
* Pillows

The invention of [metallurgy](/wiki/Metallurgy "Metallurgy") was the most important, as it quickened the pace of invention, widened the scope of human endeavour, stimulated the advancement of arts and crafts, and awakened courage and the spirit of great adventure. The search for copper was to become the most important factor in the universal spread of civilisation. Prospectors settled in foreign countries and introduced agriculture, burial customs, and their religion as well.

At first, Smith remained vague on the reasons for the spread of Egyptian influence to places without mineral deposits like [Polynesia](/wiki/Polynesia "Polynesia"). However, in 1915 [William James Perry](/wiki/W._J._Perry "W. J. Perry"), a professor of comparative religion at the [University of Manchester](/wiki/University_of_Manchester "University of Manchester"), advanced the view that the "megalith-builders" were looking for [pearls](/wiki/Pearl "Pearl") and [precious stones](/wiki/Gemstone "Gemstone"), which Smith adopted as well.

Smith did not believe that the spread of [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture") was necessarily connected to a certain race, in contrast to other diffusionists, like the German prehistorian [Gustaf Kossinna](/wiki/Gustaf_Kossinna "Gustaf Kossinna"). While he saw a racial affinity between the Egyptians and the first agriculturalists of southern Europe, both being of the "brown race," the spread of civilisation was mainly a spread of [ideas](/wiki/Idea "Idea"), not of [tribes](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe") or people.

However, in *The Ancient Egyptians and their Influence Upon the Civilization of Europe*, written in 1911, he clearly demonstrates a steep rise in "Asiatic traits (Armenoid, Alpine, Celtic)," within the Egyptian aristocracy, to Dynastic Egypt itself (among other pre-historical phenomena).

In early 1923, Smith wrote a series of articles for *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* regarding the [discovery of the tomb of Tutankhamun](/wiki/Discovery_of_the_tomb_of_Tutankhamun "Discovery of the tomb of Tutankhamun"). Those articles deal with the condition of royal and other mummies that were found before 1922.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1923a-18)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1923b-19)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1923c-20) Smith stated that Tutankhamen "narrowly escaped" the fate of being "hacked to pieces" by robbers, as was the case with the mummy of [Amenhotep II](/wiki/Amenhotep_II "Amenhotep II").[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1923b-19)

#### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_Elliot_Smith&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

In the age of [Colonialism](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism"), hyperdiffusionism proved attractive, as it showed how [missionaries](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary"), engineers and prospectors had spread civilisation all over the earth, as the colonial nations believed that they were doing themselves.

Later on, hyperdiffusionism supplied a single simple explanation of the complex process of neolithisation that made it attractive to amateur archaeologists worldwide. It could be used to retain a [Eurocentric](/wiki/Eurocentrism "Eurocentrism") view on history in the face of increasing evidence for impressive autochthonous development, such as in Zimbabwe ([Great Zimbabwe](/wiki/Great_Zimbabwe "Great Zimbabwe")), Polynesia ([Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island")), and Micronesia ([Nan Madol](/wiki/Nan_Madol "Nan Madol") on the island of [Pohnpei](/wiki/Pohnpei "Pohnpei")).

Now, it is widely believed that the megalithic graves of [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), northern [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), and [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") are much older than the Egyptian pyramids, and the Mesoamerican pyramids are more recent and considered to be local cultural innovations.

## Private life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_Elliot_Smith&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Private life")]

His father had migrated to [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") from London. He had attended a working men's college under [John Ruskin](/wiki/John_Ruskin "John Ruskin") and later became a teacher and headmaster in [Grafton](/wiki/Grafton,_New_South_Wales "Grafton, New South Wales"), New South Wales. His older brother (Stephen H. Smith) was later Director of Education in New South Wales; his younger brother (Stewart Arthur Smith) was Acting Professor of Anatomy at the University of Sydney.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

He married Kathleen Macreadie in 1902 just before moving to Cairo. During his time in London, he lived variously in Hampstead, Gower Street, and at [Regent's Park](/wiki/Regent%27s_Park "Regent's Park"). During his London years, he became a friend of [W. H. R. Rivers](/wiki/W._H._R._Rivers "W. H. R. Rivers").

Smith's youngest son, Stephen Smith, died in an accident in 1936. Smith spent his final year in a nursing home in London.

Grafton Smith died on [New Year's Day](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Day "New Year's Day") 1 January 1937 at [Broadstairs](/wiki/Broadstairs "Broadstairs") in [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Bibliography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_Elliot_Smith&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Bibliography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/The_evolution_of_the_dragon_%281919%29_%2814765679542%29.jpg/250px-The_evolution_of_the_dragon_%281919%29_%2814765679542%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_evolution_of_the_dragon_(1919)_(14765679542).jpg)

Hathor (fig. 18 in *The Evolution of the Dragon*)

[Warren Dawson](/wiki/Egyptologist "Egyptologist")'s list of Smith's publications includes 434 publications. Among the most important are:

* The Natural Subdivision of the Cerebral Hemisphere (1901).
* The Primary Subdivisions of the Mammalian Cerebellum (1902).
* The Ancient Egyptians and the origin of Civilization (London/New York, Harper & Brother 1911).
* Catalogue of the Royal Mummies in the Museum of Cairo (Cairo 1912).
* [The Egyptian Mummies](https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=ia.ark:/13960/s2r6nq3xqc6&seq=9) (with Warren R Dawson (New York, 1924)
* [The Migrations of Early Culture](https://archive.org/details/migrationsofearl00smitrich) (1915)
* On the Significance of the geographical distribution of Mummification – a study of the migrations of peoples and the spread of certain customs and beliefs (1916).
* [The Evolution of the Dragon](https://archive.org/details/evolutionofdrago00smituoft) (1919)
* (with [T. H. Pear](/wiki/Tom_Hatherley_Pear "Tom Hatherley Pear")) [Shell Shock and its Lessons](https://archive.org/details/shellshockitsles00smitiala) (1917, 2nd edition)
* [Tutankhamen and the Discovery of his Tomb](https://archive.org/details/tutankhamendisco00smituoft) (1923)
* Evolution of Man: Essays (1924, 2nd edition 1927)
* Human History (1930)
* The Diffusion of Culture (London, Watts 1933)
* [Elephants and Ethnologists](https://archive.org/details/ElephantsAndEthnologists) (1924)
* [Serle, Percival](/wiki/Percival_Serle "Percival Serle") (1949). ["Smith, Grafton Elliot"](http://gutenberg.net.au/ebooks15/1500721h/0-dict-biogSa-Sp.html#smith4). *[Dictionary of Australian Biography](/wiki/Dictionary_of_Australian_Biography "Dictionary of Australian Biography")*. Sydney: [Angus & Robertson](/wiki/Angus_%26_Robertson "Angus & Robertson").
* A. P. Elkin/N. W. G. Macintosh, Grafton Elliot Smith, The Man and his Work (Sydney University Press 1974)
* W. R. Dawson, Sir Grafton Elliot Smith: a Biographical Record by his Colleagues (London, Cape 1938)
